summary: Gruber responds to Jean-Louis Gassée's column about Swatch Group's decision to create their own smartwatch OS, agreeing it's doomed but going further: the Swiss watch industry shouldn't try to compete in smartwatches at all. He argues that if Swatch insists on smartwatches, Android Wear is the only sensible platform choice, since building a homegrown OS requires ongoing software engineering expertise the company lacks. But his core argument is that Swiss watchmakers should double down on mechanical watches, which offer something fundamentally different from computerized devices. He notes that watch collectors own multiple mechanical watches but only one smartwatch, making mechanical watches the natural complement to smartwatches rather than competitors.
key_points: Swatch Group's homegrown smartwatch OS is almost certainly doomed, and if they insist on smartwatches, Android Wear is the only viable path
Smartwatches are computer platforms that require frequent updates and bug fixes — something outside Swiss watchmakers' core competence
Watch aficionados own multiple watches, but smartwatch users only actively use one — every additional watch in a collector's rotation will be mechanical
Swiss watchmakers should position mechanical watches as an analog refuge from digitization rather than trying to out-engineer Apple
money_quotes: I love computers (duh), but I find mechanical watches to be a source of joy, a bulwark against the ever-encroaching computerization of everything.
That's not how computer platforms work, and make no mistake, smartwatches are computer platforms.
Almost no one uses multiple smartwatches.
For watch fans who actually do want multiple watches and a smartwatch, every watch other than their one smartwatch is likely to be a mechanical.
I don't think the Swiss watch industry has a chance of out-computer-engineering Apple.
Instead they should focus on what they've always done: designing and making great mechanical watches — creating a breath of analog fresh air in an ever-more-digitized world.
key_insight: The Swiss watch industry's best competitive strategy against smartwatches isn't to build worse computers, but to lean into the unique appeal of mechanical watches as analog objects in an increasingly digital world.
